punchline: So now I have two mortgages.
candidates:
REASONING: The core surprise is that a mundane financial burden (a second mortgage) arrives as the anticlimactic conclusion to a story that seemed to be building toward something emotional, dramatic, or absurd — the word "mortgage" is forbidden from the setup. The relatable situation is someone recounting a chain of reasonable decisions that each made sense in isolation, Hedberg-style, until the accumulated logic produces an absurd result. The arc is Norm-style: establish apparent stakes (something personal, consequential, meaningful), build detail that seems to point somewhere profound, then deflate it entirely with a bureaucratic financial outcome.

---
JOKE 1:
I saw a house I liked more than my house. I thought about it for a few weeks. I talked to my wife. We agreed the feeling wasn't going away. We said, you know what, life is short.

So now I have two mortgages.

---
JOKE 2:
My therapist told me I needed to stop running from my problems and start moving toward the things that make me feel safe. She said, what makes you feel safe? I said, honestly, my house. She said, then invest in that feeling.

So now I have two mortgages.

---
JOKE 3:
A man I'd never met came to my door and said he was in trouble and needed help, and I thought, what kind of person turns someone away. We talked for a while. He was going through a difficult time. He needed somewhere to stay. I told him I had a place nearby that was sitting empty.

So now I have two mortgages.
selected_joke:
My therapist told me I needed to stop running from my problems and start moving toward the things that make me feel safe. She said, what makes you feel safe? I said, honestly, my house. She said, then invest in that feeling.

So now I have two mortgages.

punchline: And that's why I'm no longer allowed at the aquarium.
candidates:
REASONING: The core surprise is a mundane, bureaucratic consequence — being banned from a public place — applied to someone who presumably did something bizarre or chaotic involving aquatic life; the setup should never hint at the ban, only at the strange escalating behavior that led to it. A relatable entry point works well here: genuine enthusiasm or a reasonable-sounding plan that slowly reveals itself as deeply misguided. The arc is Norm-style misdirection — build apparent sincerity and stakes, then deflate with institutional consequence that reframes everything as a single cohesive disaster.

---
JOKE 1:
I really just wanted to return the fish. I had bought it as a gift, she didn't want it, and the aquarium seemed like the most responsible place to release it back into the ecosystem. I had four more in my coat.

And that's why I'm no longer allowed at the aquarium.

---
JOKE 2:
The whale shark was just sitting there, doing nothing, and I thought — someone should be timing this. So I started keeping a log. Daily visits, a clipboard, a folding chair I brought from home. After three weeks I had enough data to present my findings to the other visitors, which I did, using a microphone I also brought from home.

And that's why I'm no longer allowed at the aquarium.

---
JOKE 3:
I met a woman by the jellyfish tank and we talked for about an hour. It was a genuine connection — the kind you don't want to end. So when she said she had to go, I asked for her number, she declined, and I thought: the reasonable thing to do here is to come back every day until she returns. I did this for six weeks. She worked there.

And that's why I'm no longer allowed at the aquarium.

So when she said she had to go, I asked for her number, she declined, and I thought: the reasonable thing to do here is to come back every day until she returns. I did this for six weeks. She worked there.

And that's why I'm no longer allowed at the aquarium.

punchline: Turns out it was just a regular dog.
candidates:
REASONING: The core surprise is the anticlimax — something presented with weight and mystery is revealed to be completely ordinary, specifically a "dog." The setup should build expectation of something extraordinary, strange, or paranormal, so the audience is tracking toward a revelation, not toward the word "dog" at all. The arc is classic Norm: establish stakes and apparent significance, let the listener invest in the mystery, then deflate everything with a single ordinary noun.

---
JOKE 1:
I spent three weeks in the woods of Oregon interviewing witnesses, reviewing footage, and consulting with two independent wildlife experts. I filed a forty-page report with the regional field office.

Turns out it was just a regular dog.

---
JOKE 2:
My neighbor kept saying there was something in her yard that didn't belong — something that moved wrong, that the other animals wouldn't go near, that her kids refused to look at directly. She said it had been there for a week and she was genuinely frightened. I told her I'd come over and handle it.

Turns out it was just a regular dog.

---
JOKE 3:
There's a creature that's been appearing at the edge of my property every night at the same time. It doesn't make a sound. It just stands there, watching the house. I've lost sleep over it. My wife thinks I'm losing my mind. I set up a camera.

Turns out it was just a regular dog.
selected_joke:

punchline: He said, 'That's not what I meant by cashback.'
candidates:
REASONING: The core surprise is the literal interpretation of "cashback" — the audience expects a financial/retail context, but the punchline reveals a physical return of cash in some absurd or unexpected form. A mundane setup about shopping or a transaction would naturally lead here without naming the word "cashback" until the punchline lands it. The arc works best as a slow build of reasonable context — someone doing something that seems generous or practical — that gets deflated when the word reframes the whole situation as a misunderstanding.

---
JOKE 1:
My dad asked me to handle his grocery run. I bought everything on the list, got home, and handed him the change in a small envelope with a bow on it because I thought the presentation would be nice. He seemed confused at first, then a little moved, and then just annoyed.
He said, that's not what I meant by cashback.

---
JOKE 2:
I was at the register and the cashier asked if I wanted cashback. I said yes. She asked how much. I said surprise me. She did not surprise me. She gave me twenty dollars, which I then handed back to her because I felt the gesture deserved reciprocation.
She called her manager over and he said, that's not what I meant by cashback.

---
JOKE 3:
I used to work at a bank. One of my responsibilities was processing refund requests. A man came in very upset, said he'd been overcharged, and demanded his money back immediately. I told him I understood completely. I reached into my own wallet and gave him forty dollars out of personal solidarity.
He said, that's not what I meant by cashback.
selected_joke:

He said, that's not what I meant by cashback.

punchline: I didn't even know you could return a tattoo.
candidates:
REASONING: The core surprise is the word "return" — treating a tattoo like a consumer product you can take back to a store, which the audience never sees coming because tattoos are permanent. The mundane situation is regretting a tattoo and doing something about it, which could be framed as a perfectly normal errand until the punchline reframes it as retail. The arc is Norm-style: build the story with apparent stakes (regret, decision, outcome), then deflate it by revealing the resolution was something absurdly transactional.

---
JOKE 1:
My brother got a tattoo of his girlfriend's name on his forearm. They broke up three weeks later. He was pretty upset about it for a while, then one day he just seemed fine. I asked him what happened.
He said he didn't even know you could return a tattoo.

---
JOKE 2:
I had a tattoo on my wrist for about six years. It wasn't a bad tattoo. It just stopped being accurate. Eventually I went back to the shop and explained the situation to the guy at the counter, and he was actually very understanding about the whole thing.
I didn't even know you could return a tattoo.

---
JOKE 3:
I've been trying to make smarter decisions lately. So when I got home and thought about the tattoo, I found the receipt, I looked up their policy online, I drove back to the place, and I waited in line.
I didn't even know you could return a tattoo.

punchline: Apparently that's not what 'bring your own bag' means.
candidates:
REASONING: The core surprise is that the speaker misunderstood a policy phrase in a way that reveals something embarrassing — the forbidden key word is "misinterpretation." A mundane, relatable situation like grocery shopping or an event with a bag policy would naturally lead someone to make a reasonable but wrong assumption about what to bring. The arc is Norm-style: establish a confident, detailed account of a decision that seemed logical, then deflate it entirely with the revelation that the logic was wrong all along.

---
JOKE 1:
I've been going to the farmers market every Sunday for about three months. I always bring my dog. He fits perfectly in this canvas tote I have — he's a small dog, very calm, doesn't mind it at all. The vendors seemed a little tense about it but I figured that was just their vibe. Last week someone from the market association came over and explained things to me at some length.

Apparently that's not what "bring your own bag" means.

---
JOKE 2:
I was very prepared for my first pottery class. The supply list said bring your own bag, so I showed up with a sandbag — fifty pounds of play sand, hardware store, seven dollars. I thought, this is going to go well for me. I'm going to be ahead of everyone here.

Apparently that's not what "bring your own bag" means.

---
JOKE 3:
I had a good run at the self-checkout. Four, maybe five months of just walking out. I wasn't being careless about it — I always brought a bag, I always looked focused, I was systematic. I genuinely believed there was some kind of honor system in place that I had correctly identified and was participating in.

Apparently that's not what "bring your own bag" means.

The vendors seemed a little tense about it but I figured that was just their vibe. Last week someone from the market association came over and explained things to me at some length.

punchline: The worst part is, the horse was right.
candidates:
REASONING: The core surprise is that the horse — an animal — made a correct judgment, which reframes the setup as a story where the human was actually wrong; the word "horse" must never appear until the punchline. The mundane situation is someone recounting an embarrassing moment where they were overconfident or dismissive, only to be humbled — the relatable feeling of being corrected by someone you shouldn't have ignored. The arc is Norm-style: build apparent stakes around a reasonable human complaint or decision, then deflate it entirely by revealing the source of the correction was an animal whose assessment turned out to be accurate.

---
JOKE 1:
I got into a pretty heated argument at my cousin's farm last summer. I won't get into the full details, but it started over whether I could clear a fence, it escalated, and several things were said that I'm not proud of. I stood my ground, though. I felt I had made my position very clear.

The worst part is, the horse was right.

---
JOKE 2:
I don't take criticism well. Never have. My therapist says it comes from a need to feel competent, and I said, with respect, I think you're projecting. I'm actually a very competent person. I have a whole list of moments that prove it.

The worst part is, the horse was right.

---
JOKE 3:
There's this look people give you when they think you're making a mistake — like they're watching something happen that they've already decided not to intervene in. I saw that look a lot growing up. From teachers, from coaches, from my father. But the one time I really should have listened, I didn't.

The worst part is, the horse was right.

punchline: And the landlord said, 'That's not a pet deposit.'
candidates:
REASONING: The core surprise is that something the tenant assumed was a pet deposit is being corrected — the forbidden word is "pet," and the joke hinges on what unusual thing the tenant brought in that prompted this clarification. A relatable setup would involve moving into a new apartment and navigating the bureaucratic awkwardness of deposits, fees, and rules with a landlord. The arc builds through mundane rental logistics before deflating into the landlord having to specify, with weary professionalism, that whatever amount was handed over does not fall under that particular category.

---
JOKE 1:
I moved into my new place last month. The landlord was very thorough — went over every clause, every fee, what was and wasn't covered. Then I handed him three hundred dollars and explained it was for the raccoon.

And the landlord said, that's not a pet deposit.

---
JOKE 2:
I've been keeping a horse in my studio apartment for about six weeks now. I want to be clear: I disclosed this upfront. I filled out the form, I checked the box, I handed over the additional fee. The landlord looked at the check for a long time.

And the landlord said, that's not a pet deposit.

---
JOKE 3:
My landlord is a very reasonable man. When I moved in, he sat me down and said the building had a strict no-pets policy, but that he was willing to make exceptions on a case-by-case basis for a standard deposit. So I brought him an envelope with my documentation, a letter of reference from my previous landlord, and forty thousand dollars in cash.

And the landlord said, that's not a pet deposit.

And the landlord said, that's not a pet deposit.

punchline: So technically, I'm employee of the month.
candidates:
REASONING: The core surprise is unearned achievement — the speaker claims a workplace honor through a technicality rather than merit, which the audience doesn't see coming because the setup should frame something that looks like failure or absurdity. A mundane situation like being the only person in a category, or surviving some kind of elimination, would naturally lead to a hollow claim of distinction. The arc works best as a Norm-style slow build where apparent context and detail make the punchline land as a deflation — the "achievement" reframes everything that came before as a kind of accidental victory.

---
JOKE 1:
Everyone at the distribution center quit in October. All fifteen of them, in the same week. Something about conditions. I don't know, I wasn't really paying attention because I was at home with a pretty bad cold.

So technically, I'm employee of the month.

---
JOKE 2:
I've been driving for a ride-share app for three years. In that time I've received over four hundred ratings. The vast majority of them are bad. One of them is just a photograph of me, which I didn't know was possible. But the rest — and there are eleven of them — are five stars.

So technically, I'm employee of the month.

---
JOKE 3:
My company has a rule that you can only be nominated for employee of the month by a direct supervisor. And for a long time I thought that was going to be a problem for me, because I had gone almost two years without speaking to mine. But then we got a new one, and on his very first day I introduced myself, shook his hand, and looked him in the eyes.

So technically, I'm employee of the month.

punchline: They said it was the most polite robbery they'd ever seen.
candidates:
REASONING: The core surprise is that an act of extreme courtesy is being assessed by robbery victims or police — the word "robbery" reframes everything preceding it as criminal rather than merely awkward. The setup should establish someone with compulsive over-politeness in a situation that feels mundane until the stakes are revealed. The arc is Norm-style misdirection: build a portrait of someone admirably, even enviably courteous, then deflate it entirely by revealing the context was a crime.

---
JOKE 1:
My grandfather always said if you're going to ask someone for something, you look them in the eye, you say please, and you wait for an answer before you proceed. He also said you should always hold the door, offer to carry bags, and never raise your voice under any circumstances. He passed those values down to my father, my father passed them down to me, and apparently my father also passed them down to my uncle, because when they reviewed the security footage,
they said it was the most polite robbery they'd ever seen.

---
JOKE 2:
I always make sure to thank people. The cashier, the bus driver, whoever holds the elevator — I say thank you, genuinely, every time. I think it costs nothing and it means a lot. Last Tuesday I was running low on cash and needed to make something work, so I kept the whole thing very calm, very respectful, made eye contact, said please and thank you at each stage of the interaction. The detective who reviewed the tape said
it was the most polite robbery they'd ever seen.

---
JOKE 3:
There's a man in my town who everyone agrees has exceptional manners. He writes thank-you notes. He remembers names. When he disagrees with you, he waits until you're completely finished speaking before he explains why you're wrong, which is somehow worse but also more impressive. He recently had some financial difficulties, and rather than let them change who he is as a person, he stayed true to his values throughout the entire ordeal. The responding officers noted that
it was the most polite robbery they'd ever seen.
selected_joke:

He recently had some financial difficulties, and rather than let them change who he is as a person, he stayed true to his values throughout the entire ordeal. The responding officers noted that
it was the most polite robbery they'd ever seen.

punchline: Now every time I hear a doorbell, I flinch.
candidates:
REASONING: The core surprise is a trauma/conditioned response, but the source of that trauma should be something mundane and slightly absurd rather than anything genuinely serious — the audience expects either a dramatic backstory or a simple gag, not something that earns the Pavlovian flinch through accumulated, deadpan detail. A relatable situation involving repeated, unwanted interruptions — like a job, a living situation, or a hobby — could naturally produce this response without telegraphing the punchline. The arc is Norm-style: build stakes and context around something ordinary, let the detail accumulate until the flinch feels not only earned but inevitable, then land it as a flat, reasonable conclusion.

---
JOKE 1:
I used to do product testing for a smart doorbell company. They needed someone to ring it. Just ring it, over and over, for eight hours a day, so the engineers could check the sensitivity. I did that job for eleven months.
Now every time I hear a doorbell, I flinch.

---
JOKE 2:
I lived next door to a man who was very popular. I don't mean that as a compliment. I mean people came to his door at all hours — friends, cousins, food delivery, more cousins. The walls were thin. This went on for three years. I moved across town. I thought that would help.
Now every time I hear a doorbell, I flinch.

---
JOKE 3:
I was in a play once. Small production, community theater. My only job was to press a button offstage that triggered the doorbell sound effect whenever an actor approached the door. The director felt the timing was never quite right. We rehearsed for four months. The run was six weekends. He still wasn't satisfied.
Now every time I hear a doorbell, I flinch.
selected_joke:
I was in a play once. Small production, community theater. My only job was to press a button offstage that triggered the doorbell sound effect whenever an actor approached the door. The director felt the timing was never quite right. We rehearsed for four months. The run was six weekends. He still wasn't satisfied.
Now every time I hear a doorbell, I flinch.

punchline: Which is weird because I don't even own a boat.
candidates:
REASONING: The core surprise is the non-sequitur disconnect between whatever problem is being described and the completely irrelevant disclaimer about boat ownership — the audience expects either a resolution or an escalation, not a denial of nautical involvement. The setup should establish a mundane, specific complaint or situation (something going wrong in ordinary life) that makes the boat disclaimer land as a bewildering but earnest clarification. The arc is Norm-style misdirection: build a real-seeming situation with detail and apparent stakes, then deflate it entirely by pivoting to something that reframes the whole thing as somehow boat-adjacent without ever explaining why.

---
JOKE 1:
My neighbor came over last week very upset, said I'd been leaving things on his property, that it was disrespectful and a pattern of behavior and he was tired of it. We talked for about forty-five minutes. At the end he asked me to please stop storing my anchor there, which is weird because I don't even own a boat.

---
JOKE 2:
I've been getting a lot of mail lately that isn't addressed to me. Different names, different addresses, nothing in common. I brought it to the post office and the woman behind the counter looked through the stack for a while and said, sir, all of these are for the same vessel, which is weird because I don't even own a boat.

---
JOKE 3:
I went to the doctor because my back had been hurting for a few months. She asked a lot of questions, did the whole exam, typed everything into her computer. Then she looked at me very seriously and said, I have to be honest with you, this is consistent with someone who is mooring incorrectly, which is weird because I don't even own a boat.

punchline: He looked me dead in the eye and said, 'Sir, this is a Wendy's.'
candidates:
REASONING: The core surprise is that an increasingly serious or dramatic buildup — something that feels like a confession, confrontation, or existential moment — is deflated by the mundane corporate reality of fast food customer service. The forbidden key word is "Wendy's," which should never appear in the setup. The arc follows Norm Macdonald's method: build genuine stakes and emotional weight, then collapse them entirely with the reminder that none of it matters because of where we are.

---
JOKE 1:
I've been going through a lot lately. Lost my job, my wife left, I've been sleeping in my car. Last Tuesday I just needed someone to talk to, so I pulled up to the drive-through window and started opening up. Really getting into it. The guy on the other end was quiet for a long time.
He looked me dead in the eye and said, sir, this is a Wendy's.

---
JOKE 2:
I had been rehearsing the speech for weeks. I had notes. I told him that our friendship had become one-sided, that I felt unseen, that I needed him to acknowledge the pattern. I was calm. I was clear. I thought it went well.
He looked me dead in the eye and said, sir, this is a Wendy's.

---
JOKE 3:
I'm not a confrontational person, but I had finally had enough. I walked up to the counter, I laid out my grievances one by one, I cited specific dates, I remained composed. When I finished, there was a long pause.
He looked me dead in the eye and said, sir, this is a Wendy's.

He looked me dead in the eye and said, sir, this is a Wendy's.

punchline: And I still don't know whose cat that was.
candidates:
REASONING: The core surprise is that a cat appeared, became central to some event, and then vanished without explanation — the forbidden key word is "ownership." A mundane situation like helping a neighbor, attending a family gathering, or dealing with a household disruption could naturally lead to an unexplained animal presence. The arc works best as a Norm-style slow build: establish stakes and a seemingly significant memory, pile on detail, then deflate it all by revealing the central mystery was never the drama but a background animal whose origin remains unsolved.

---
JOKE 1:
My grandmother's funeral was the most important day of our family's life. Fifty people. A priest. My uncle gave a speech that made grown men cry. At the reception, my aunt's casserole ran out and there was a moment of real tension. We released white doves. One of them didn't fly away, which everyone agreed was a sign.
And I still don't know whose cat that was.

---
JOKE 2:
I moved into my apartment and immediately felt at home. Unpacked everything. Set up the bed. Cooked my first meal there — pasta, nothing fancy. Watched a movie. Fell asleep on the couch.
And I still don't know whose cat that was.

---
JOKE 3:
I've been in therapy for three years now. We've covered my childhood, my relationship with my father, a recurring dream about a hallway that my therapist says is significant. Last month she told me I was making real progress. She said she was proud of me.
And I still don't know whose cat that was.

punchline: That's when I realized the interview had started twenty minutes ago.
candidates:
REASONING: The core surprise is that the candidate has unknowingly been *evaluated* the whole time they thought they were just making small talk or waiting — the word "interview" must never appear in the setup. A mundane relatable situation is arriving early for a job interview, chatting casually with someone in the lobby or hallway, feeling comfortable and unguarded. The arc is Norm-style: build the scene with specific, grounding detail that makes the protagonist seem competent and self-aware, then deflate everything with the realization that their relaxed, unfiltered behavior was the interview all along.

---
JOKE 1:
I'd been talking to the guy in the lobby for a while. Told him about the job I got fired from, the one before that, why I don't really believe in references as a concept. He was very easy to talk to. Very non-threatening energy. Offered me a coffee. I said I don't drink coffee because I don't like to depend on things.
That's when I realized the interview had started twenty minutes ago.

---
JOKE 2:
I was waiting in the hallway and a man came and sat next to me. I assumed he was also waiting, so I just started talking. Told him the salary range I'd accept, the one below which I'd consider an insult, what I thought of the company's recent restructuring, my honest read on the CEO based on his LinkedIn photo.
That's when I realized the interview had started twenty minutes ago.

---
JOKE 3:
The woman at the front desk was very friendly, so I just kept talking to her. She kept asking follow-up questions, which I appreciated. I said I work best independently, that I find most managers redundant, and that the main thing I'm looking for is a place that won't waste my time. She nodded and wrote something down.
That's when I realized the interview had started twenty minutes ago.
